Specifically, it would seen to be diametrically opposite to

I am a Eather of 5 and rny business is my sole neans of
income. While we retain other professionals to examine. review,
and stamp many of our p1ans, those professionals are not
responsible for day to day contact. Liniting ny contact with staff
except as initiated by staff wou1d greatly curtail my ability to
provide needed income for my tamily.
Business in the City of Banning is about 19t of our overall
business and any abridgement of our normal processing routine would
seem to me to exhaust our profitability 
and cause extreme distress
to myself, my family and my constituency.
I cannot afford an attorney in this matter, nor do I think it
is warranted, since this entire affair was generated by a statement
of our city ltanager which your letter put aside entirely. 
I arn
hopeful that your advice will be a little 
more specific after you
have reviewed these docurnents. Naturally I an totally at your
service, my fites and my business activities are certainly open to
your perusal on an as needed and voluntary basis.
Thank you for your concern

sections.question_synthetic: Terry Holbert, a City of Banning councilmember and surveying/engineering professional, seeks clarification on an FPPC advice letter (R-89-443) regarding the use of the word 'processing' in the Conclusion section and its relationship to Section 18700.1(b)(4), and requests confirmation that the City Manager's statement prohibiting his surveying work in the city was addressed in the FPPC's response.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence from the public to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document references a prior FPPC advice letter (R-89-443 from September 11, 1989) but does not contain the FPPC's original advice. Multiple copies of similar letters appear in the document. No clear Q&C sections present. Holbert is responding to and questioning aspects of a previous FPPC response.

embedding.summary: This document consists of correspondence from Terry Holbert, a City of Banning councilmember, responding to a previous FPPC advice letter and requesting clarification on the language used regarding 'processing' and its compatibility with conflict of interest statutes. Holbert also requests letters from the City Attorney clarifying the basis for the initial FPPC inquiry and whether the City Manager's prohibition on his surveying work within the city was upheld or denied.
